message: They can be distinct from Feminism if they choose. More often, the MRM is a haven for contrarian antifeminists. Therefore: the MRM has a right to exist, but not as a counter force against feminism.

The MRM has valid (and invalid) points regardless of it's stance towards feminism. However, the movement has as many invalid arguments that come from a place of putting down women or reacting to progress for women. In those cases, the MRM is not a legitimate movement for equal rights, but a reactionary movement against equal rights. Only in those cases does the MRM not have a right to exist, because those are not good faith efforts for the promotion of equality.

@slow-abbey, I respect your opinion, but I disagree. The MRM has valid points, but it's not always about equality. Sometimes it's about pushing back against progress. That's when it loses its legitimacy.
"@genteel-leaf, You're dancing around the issue. Either the MRM has a right to exist or it doesn't. Can't have it both ways, can you? 😏"

Forcing of private businesses to serve persons in violation of their religious objections. Look I don't care what anyone believes; if somebody only wanted to serve Kosher food to Orthodox Jews, go for it! If the market doesn't like it or exist it will go away. I hate the idea that the government would force them to serve--whatever. Grave diggers or whoever they find unclean or whatever.

On the flip side, a goodly fraction of the Left does seem to currently be going down a dark path of (a least socially) censoring non-politically-correct speech, restricting religious liberty, and banning weapons of self-defense. 

All of which are classical Liberal values of the most paramount importance.

"@frigid-tree, I agree with you. It's a slippery slope when we start dictating what businesses can and can't do based on religious beliefs. It's their business, their rules. But @adaptive-margin, I think you're oversimplifying it. It's not just about serving kosher food, it's about discrimination. We've seen businesses refuse service to LGBTQ+ customers, for example. That's not okay."

I'll admit that I was wrong about my initial point. I guess my family just tended to end up at better paying places generally.

According to some articles I've read there is a growing trend of places getting rid of tips so I'll simply have to disagree with your last point. And again, it simply is not and never should be the responsibility of the consumer to make up for a shitty employer. Lot's of employees get shafted, and enabling the system certainly isn't going to change anything. Maybe trying to fight it won't change anything either, but I'm of the opinion that you shouldn't just give up.

>but plenty of waiters make absolute bank. My one brother who is still a waiter works at a fancy club

How many "fancy restaurants" are there compared to regular restaurants? There are undoubtedly far more regular restaurants compared to fancy restaurants and that limits the amount of jobs that can make hundreds a night. According to the bureau of labor statistics, waiters on average make $19,990/yr. message:
What does it mean to be racist?

Well, one clear example would be doing something that you know promotes racism. Saying the N word, voting for Trump, fighting for the Confederates. Racism may not be the primary motivator for doing those things (really stretching by saying that, but we will pretend for a second) but doing those things furthers racism, and so someone has to do them *in spite* of racism. However, to be ok with racism is in and of itself racist.

Right, but that refusal to take action is a choice, on both accounts. If you were willing to sacrifice EVERYTHING else in your life- all your time, energy, relationships, moral convictions, etc- to have 5 billion dollars, you might not end up having five billion dollars, but chances are very good you'd be what most people consider to be "rich".

Fat people, by convincing themselves it's impossible, are really only saying that they aren't willing to extend EVERY LAST OUNCE of their physical, mental, and psychological ability into eating less and moving more. And though they might have to extend much more energy into keeping themselves away from overeating than a thin person, it's still not an excuse, IMO.

Wanting it is only half the battle though.  I really want 5 billion dollars, in fact I would argue that I want it as much as anybody else.  However, I don't take action by convincing myself that its an impossible task not worth attempting.  Its probably the same concept for fat people.

>Granted it is often difficult to prove that something was a deliberate deception rather than innocently being wrong...

That's where your proposition breaks down.

In the United States, at least, some forms of misrepresentation and defamation are already treated as torts.  Or even criminal offenses, in some cases.  You've got perjury, fraud, embezzlement, slander, libel, false advertising...There are already many charges, familiar in American legal culture, that involve deception.

To classify "the deliberate spread of misinformation" as a criminal offense, across the board, would absolutely hobble any country's criminal justice system.  Imagine yourself trying to contest a speeding ticket, and being forced to wait 18 months, as you join the end of a long line, mostly comprised of people bickering about who lied to whom.  

While no one is likely to try to tell you that "lying is good," or anything like that, I don't think jammed courts are something you really want.

That's not at all what I'm suggesting... I'm talking about deliberate manipulation of data in order to spread false information. For an example see the one I gave in my OP about someone who edited a photo of Obama to make him appear stupid, or the researcher who falsified data and lied about the link between vaccines and autism. You cannot accidentally do these things, and there is no question about whether or not they are false and deliberate.

If your worries are based on the Kavanaugh accusations, what difference would it make to leave teaching? Kavanaugh is not being accused based on anything he did in his job - he is being accused based on his behavior at parties in high school and college. 

Did you go to parties in high school and college? Is that going to change if you change professions? Leaving teaching is not going to insulate you from false accusations - that can happen to anyone at any time, whether you’re an older brother being punished for your little sister’s misbehavior or an employee fired for your manager’s misjudgment - or you could be a dark-skinned US President accused of being born in Africa, a college lacrosse player that hired the wrong stripper, or an innocent man serving life in prison for a murder you did not do. 

Our legal systems are not perfect and our society is not perfect. We live in a broken world where there is pain, injustice, and false accusations. Don’t let that keep you from doing a job that you love.

message:
Gender has always been linked to our sex, if you were born male you were a man and if you were born female a woman. Throughout history and this has always been the case.  

I don't believe you can identify as a gender, but believe you want to conform to a set of rules imposed by gender roles.

What was or still is toxic is gender stereotypes and the definition of femininity and masculinity that imposes rules on the sexes. 

By applying female gender roles and calling themselves women for it, trans women are reinforcing harmful female stereotypes.

Not liking make up and not wearing dresses doesn't make me less of a woman than one that does nor does it make me confused about my gender.

There used to be no such thing as sexual orientation. There was no straight and gay, there were just normal people and people with sexual deviancies or perversions. Alan Turing, for example, would be considered a straight man with a taboo proclivity. Your sex carried certain inherent implications. If you had X and Y chromosomes, you are a man and you are meant to be attracted to women. This was considered "well-established." And this was in part because straight people weren't *aware* of being straight. They simply grew up and did what came naturally to them.

Then we started to learn that sexual orientation is an innate part of human psychology, and that it doesn't always "match." So being a man didn't necessarily mean that you were going to be attracted to women.

Now we're learning that there is also a such thing as gender identity. And again, it doesn't always "match." So you can be born with X and Y chromosomes but your gender identity may not be "man." Again, this can be hard for a cis person to grasp. You might not *notice* your gender identity because it's normal to you, and it's normal to society. But it's still there, and it always has been. It just hasn't been acknowledged in your society until now.

@genteel-leaf, you're missing the point entirely. Gender identity isn't about conforming to roles, it's about how one feels and identifies. You're comparing apples and oranges here. And @golden-taxi, while I appreciate the history lesson, we're not in the 50s anymore. People are more than their chromosomes and attractions.
personality_characteristics: ['Healthy', 'Democrat', 'Fascinated with the latest health and fitness gadgets']

message:
> I don't think we suffer at all because we have the freedom to stop being exploited. 

Really? And how is that? If only people like you were there for the Chinese children being exploited. They could just walk away! They have the power! Right?

>That kid in bangladesh could stop going to the factory.

And have no income to pay the little food he can afford. Good one, you're telling a kid to walk to his death.

>The sailors of that ship could never return to it when it goes to port

Turns out, as with the kid example, it's much more complicated than that.

>yet they are unwilling to fight for freedom.

False. Animals do fight for their freedom and moreso than humans do. They are less *capable* of fighting for their freedom because they don't know how metal cages work. And for the few who are smart enough to figure out how to escape, you sure ya betcha they try everything in their power to do flee. 

Or, you know, there's the whole fight-flight-freeze response in most land species and a huge bunch of sea creatures as well, specifically developed evolutionary to get out of threatening situations and thus to fight for their freedom.

Is fighting against straw men arguments your day to day job?
